"l,Ieaning of Foreseeably"
Thank you for your instructive nenorandum. Your cotrments therein
reinforce the Conflicts of Interest Division view that recent
Comrnission actions reqarding clarification 
of rec{uire:nents of
conflict of interest codes and alterations of requlations in 2
California Administrative Code Sectlon 18700 et seq. will have
little 
or no effect upon the staff's previous fecormuendation for
approval of the conflict of Interest code of the Franchise Tax
Board.
The Conflicts of Interest Division is prep3ring a package of
materials which outline all the CommissionS recent actions regard-
ing the developnent of conflict of interest codes. That conplete
package will be transmitted to the Franchise Tax Board on sePtember
15.
Enclosed are portions of that package for the purpose of enabling
the Board to undertake a systematic review of its proposed Code in
light of the Couimission's nevr posture. The proposed Code of the
Board will be heard at the Coramission's meeting of Septeriber 21
and 22, 1,976. f wil,l be in touch with you for the purpose of dis-
cussing any proposed changes which you may wish to institute 
Ln
light of the conEoission's determinations. A eopy of your memoran-
dun will be made a part of the record of review of the Code for
the Franchise Tax Board.

Memorondum
To , Delbert spurlock, chief
conflicts of Interest Divi s ion
Fair Political Practices Commi ssion

Sacramento, CA 958L4
From : 
Jack E. Gordon
Subiect: Meaning of " Foreseeably"
Fron(hise rdx Boqrd
FPPC

41O : JEG : ah
The past several meetings of the commission have 1ed me to believe
that it has some reservations as to the propriety of including
the Franchise Tax Boardrs Auditor Irs and II!s within the classifi-
cation of designated employees under the Political 
Re form Act of
L97 4.
I wish to acknowl-edge at the outset that the Commissionrs staff
obviously did not share its concern since, by memorandum dated
June 30, 7976, tine staff found no areas of dispute in the depart-
mentrs proposed conflict of interest code.
I have reviewed the "Proposed Amendmen E't Eo 2, ca1 . Adn[n ' code
Section 18700 and submit that under this new version the depart-
ment's auditor Trs and IIts must be designated under the criteria
of partj-cipating in the making of a governmental decision.
The proposed amendment provides in part:
(c) A pubJ-ic official 
or designated employee
"participates in the making of a governmental decision"
when, acting within the authority of his or her e5€iee
position iritheu€-eigeifieaa€-eubstan€ive-review, he or
she: ...
(2) Advises or makes recommendations to the
decision-maker, either directly or without significant
intervening substantive review, by:


Delbert Spurlock, Chief
August 73, 1976
Page 2
(A) conducting research or investigations
requiri,ng which require(s) the exercise of j udgment
on the part of the offj-cial or desiqnated emplovee
and the purpose of which is to whieh-may inffuence
the decision, or
(B) preparing
analysis, or opinion,
requires the exerqi se
or presenting any report,
ora1ly or in writing, whj_ch
of j udgment on the part of
1.
)
the official 
or desiqnated employee and the purpose
of which is to whieh-rnay influence the decisionl-
The mechanics of the audit of tax returns are as follows:
Over the years certain criteria have been developed for the
selection of returns for audit.
Returns thus selected are segregated on the basis of size,
complexity and type (individual, partnership, corporation,
fiduciary, etc.) and are assigned to auditors on a random
basi s .
The auditor then ',works" the returns. Within very broad
guidelines, he decides what action is appropriate. For
example, he decides if the return deserves any action at
all. 
He decides the areas to be explored. He decides
what questions to ask and what verification to obtain.
He decides the amount of additj_onal tax (or re fund) due.
Upon conclusion of the auditorrs efforts with respect to
any return, the auditor prepares a report which contains
his reconunendation.
The auditorls report goes to the reviewer (presumably the
" deci sion-maker" mentj_oned in the proposed amendment) . 
The
reviewer checks the report for adequacy of scope of the
audit, technical accuracy and obvious errors and, if he
approves, forvrards the report for billing or re fund action.
.,
4.


Delbert Spurlock, Chie f
August 13, 7976
Page 3
Even this capsule sulunary of the audit process makes it clear
that the auditor participates in the making of a goverrunental
decisj-on, as that activity is defined in Ca1 . Admin. Code
Sectj,on 18700(c) (2). No other interpretation j-s reasonable.
There does remain, however, the question of foreseeability. 
I
have read the cornment on this subject attached to your memoran-
dum of August 9, 1976. I'flhile f cannot disagree with the corunent,
I find it of little 
practical assistance. I suggest that fore-
seeability may be viewed in either of two ways as relates to the
department within the context of a conflict of interest code.
If by " foreseeable" the Commission is only considering statis-
ticat probability, i.e., mere chance that an auditor wil-1 be
assigned a return where by his action he could materially affect
his financial interest, then probably few, if any, of our
employees should be designated. We work w-ith such a substantial
percentage of the population that the odd.s are sj-mp1y too great
to consider it foreseeable that any one auditor wil-l- be assigned
a return $/hich, by his action thereon, coul-d affect his financial
interests.
If, however, " foreseeable" refers to the possibility that any
designated position or group of designated positions might be
staffed by a person or persons who may improperly use his position
to favorably affect his financial interest, then I suggest our
auditors must be designated. Among the powers of the auditors is
the authority to generally request any return he wishes and, once
a return is in his possession, he may reconunend virtuall-y any
action he wishes. He may, for example, obtain the return of a
relative in whose business he has invested and he may "no change"
that return. There is every l-ikelihood that such action would
go unchallenged.
If, however, such an employee is designated and his business
interest j,s a matter of public record, it is submitted that he
is much less likeIy to make the improper move " And even if he
does, designation and disclosure provides a check on such actions.


Del-bert Spurlock, Chie f
August ]-3. 1976
Page 4
I submit the genesis of the Pol-itical Reform Act was the impro-
per actions of efected and appointed official-s and that the
opportunity for their improprieties came not by mere chance,
but by overt action. I suggest that vitri Ie the vast majority of
people are honest and not prone to improper activity, a few are
not. I further suggest the presence of those few is foreseeable '
Since there is no way to single out those persons who would act
improperly, an inclusj,ve designation and disclosure policy seems
appropriate.
I will appreciate Your co[unents.
!t;
Y.

i /lunuqu, , collections-A&nin. &
''

is much less likeIy to make the improper move " And even if he
does, designation and disclosure provides a check on such actions.


Del-bert Spurlock, Chie f
August ]-3. 1976
I submit the genesis of the Pol-itical Reform Act was the impro-
per actions of efected and appointed official-s and that the
opportunity for their improprieties came not by mere chance,

Since there is no way to single out those persons who would act

Y.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether Franchise Tax Board auditors should be designated as employees under conflict of interest code requirements, particularly regarding the interpretation of 'foreseeably' in relation to potential conflicts of interest.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC's Conflicts of Interest Division supports the staff's previous recommendation for approval of the Franchise Tax Board's conflict of interest code, noting that recent Commission actions and regulatory clarifications will have little effect on this recommendation. The Division is preparing materials outlining recent Commission actions on conflict of interest codes for the Board's systematic review before the September 21-22, 1976 hearing.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: This document contains two pieces: (1) FPPC's response memorandum from Spurlock dated September 1, 1976, and (2) the incoming request memorandum from Jack Gordon dated August 13, 1976. The FPPC response is brief and does not directly answer the substantive legal questions posed. The document discusses conflict of interest code designations and the 'foreseeability' standard. OCR quality appears moderate with some scanning artifacts visible.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether Franchise Tax Board auditors should be designated as employees under conflict of interest code requirements, particularly regarding the interpretation of 'foreseeably' in relation to potential conflicts of interest.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC's Conflicts of Interest Division supports the staff's previous recommendation for approval of the Franchise Tax Board's conflict of interest code, noting that recent Commission actions and regulatory clarifications will have little effect on this recommendation. The Division is preparing materials outlining recent Commission actions on conflict of interest codes for the Board's systematic review before the September 21-22, 1976 hearing.

embedding.summary: FPPC Conflicts of Interest Division Chief Delbert Spurlock responds to Jack Gordon's August 13, 1976 memorandum regarding the meaning of 'foreseeably' in conflict of interest code designations for Franchise Tax Board auditors. Spurlock indicates the Division's support for the Board's proposed code and transmits materials reflecting the Commission's recent actions on conflict of interest code development.
